title: Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media
summary: Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media uses an original HTML readiness exam scenario to retrieve earlier HTML skills, add a bounded artifact layer, and verify transfer with source and browser evidence.
objectives: Retrieve html document boilerplate, html elements nesting, html attributes, html text headings lists, html links urls, html images alt, html svg, html audio, html video, html iframe, html validation tools without step-by-step imitation.
Produce and verify a real HTML readiness exam artifact or evidence report.
Preserve earlier valid requirements while diagnosing changed content and constraints.

steps.title: Predict 1: heat-relief information hub launch desk · quiz
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim survives a changed heat-relief information hub launch desk case with incomplete visual evidence? Work context: HTML readiness exam.
steps.why: Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media requires this decision because each decision must preserve document meaning, user control, portability, and inspectable evidence. This is checkpoint 1 in the HTML readiness exam.

content.title: HTML readiness exam · evidence decision
content.text: Commit before feedback, then connect the selected claim to the HTML readiness exam evidence.

hints: Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media: first isolate the evidence required by “Predict 1: heat-relief information hub launch desk.”
Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media: compare the current artifact with the named predict goal for step 1.
Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media: use the smallest defensible change or claim that satisfies “Predict 1: heat-relief information hub launch desk.”

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 1: a visible heading supplies the browser-tab title, language, encoding, and mobile viewport automatically.
HTML readiness exam case 1: the document needs an explicit doctype, root language, head metadata, title, viewport instruction, and body content.
HTML readiness exam case 1: a body element alone is a complete production document because browsers repair every missing signal identically.

options.text: HTML readiness exam phase 2.1: choose an element that represents the outer relationship
HTML readiness exam phase 2.2: open the parent before its children
HTML readiness exam phase 2.3: complete and close each child in order
HTML readiness exam phase 2.4: close the parent after its final child

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 3: every attribute becomes more effective when given the text value true.
HTML readiness exam case 3: attribute syntax and values must follow the contract defined for that element and attribute.
HTML readiness exam case 3: any invented attribute changes native browser behavior if its name sounds descriptive.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 4: heading rank is a font-size control, so smaller-looking sections should always use h6.
HTML readiness exam case 4: select text elements for the relationship they communicate, then use CSS later for visual scale.
HTML readiness exam case 4: every sentence should be a heading because headings receive stronger default styling.

Readiness Exam: Documents, Links, Images, and Media: use the smallest defensible change or claim that satisfies “Decide 5: multi-channel help directory.”

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 5: click here is always sufficient link text because users read every surrounding sentence first.
HTML readiness exam case 5: the href must use the right URL form and the link text should identify the destination or action.
HTML readiness exam case 5: every internal project link needs the author’s full local file-system path.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 6: every image needs a long visual description, including decorative flourishes already ignored by sighted readers.
HTML readiness exam case 6: classify the image purpose first, then provide the shortest equivalent that preserves that purpose.
HTML readiness exam case 6: the filename is always an adequate alternative because it identifies the asset.

options.text: HTML readiness exam phase 7.1: decide whether the vector is content, a control, or decoration
HTML readiness exam phase 7.2: choose external image or inline SVG behavior
HTML readiness exam phase 7.3: set a viewBox that contains the drawing coordinates
HTML readiness exam phase 7.4: name meaningful vectors or hide decorative ones

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 8: once an audio file plays, controls, fallback, and a transcript are optional accessibility extras.
HTML readiness exam case 8: usable audio needs controls, supported sources, fallback, and an equivalent transcript.
HTML readiness exam case 8: a one-sentence summary is always equivalent to a detailed emergency advisory.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 9: words burned into some frames replace synchronized captions for dialogue, sound, and speaker changes.
HTML readiness exam case 9: the player needs controls, supported sources, a caption track, fallback, and equivalent text access.
HTML readiness exam case 9: muted autoplay is the only reliable way to make a public briefing accessible.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 10: the iframe title edits the heading shown inside the remote page, so it can be omitted when that page has an h1.
HTML readiness exam case 10: the parent document must name the iframe, constrain its permissions, and make its box responsive.
HTML readiness exam case 10: removing all constraints is safest because embedded content needs every browser capability.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 11: if the page looks correct in one browser, the HTML is valid and interoperable.
HTML readiness exam case 11: rendering and conformance are different evidence; validate source and trace each message to a root cause.
HTML readiness exam case 11: repair every visible symptom with extra div elements without reading validator output.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 13: indenting a paragraph beneath main makes it a child even if the tags close out of order.
HTML readiness exam case 13: parent-child relationships come from correctly nested tags; indentation should then mirror that structure.
HTML readiness exam case 13: all elements are void elements, so closing tags are optional whenever the page still appears.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 14: every attribute becomes more effective when given the text value true.
HTML readiness exam case 14: attribute syntax and values must follow the contract defined for that element and attribute.
HTML readiness exam case 14: boolean attributes are enabled only when written with the numeric value 1.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 15: heading rank is a font-size control, so smaller-looking sections should always use h6.
HTML readiness exam case 15: select text elements for the relationship they communicate, then use CSS later for visual scale.
HTML readiness exam case 15: a sequence of br elements communicates the same list relationship as ol or ul.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 16: click here is always sufficient link text because users read every surrounding sentence first.

options.text: HTML readiness exam phase 17.1: determine whether the image is informative, functional, complex, or decorative
HTML readiness exam phase 17.2: write or omit verbal content according to that purpose
HTML readiness exam phase 17.3: provide intrinsic dimensions to reserve its aspect ratio
HTML readiness exam phase 17.4: test the surrounding content with the image unavailable

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 18: path geometry automatically becomes a useful accessible name for every inline SVG.
HTML readiness exam case 18: choose an embedding method deliberately, provide a name for meaningful graphics, and hide decorative graphics.
HTML readiness exam case 18: every decorative path needs role img and a spoken title.

options.text: HTML readiness exam case 19: once an audio file plays, controls, fallback, and a transcript are optional accessibility extras.
HTML readiness exam case 19: usable audio needs controls, supported sources, fallback, and an equivalent transcript.
HTML readiness exam case 19: autoplay is required so keyboard users can discover that audio exists.
